paper_title: Excellent mechanical properties and underlined deformation twinning mechanism of a low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel

caption: Fig. 1. Microstructure of the solution-treated steel. (a) EBSD phase map. (b) EBSD KAM map. (c) EBSD IPF map along the rolling direction. (d, e, f) Selected area diffraction patterns along the [100]γ, [110]γ, and $[ 1 1 0 ] _ { \mathrm { M C } }$ zone axis, respectively.
paper_abstract: Qingfeng Kang , Zexi Zhang , Ziyuan Gao , Cunyu Wang , Jianxiong Liang , Zhengdong Liu , Yuqing Weng , Wenquan Cao D
paper_title: Excellent mechanical properties and underlined deformation twinning mechanism of a low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel
citation_sentences: The microstructure of the starting materials after solution-treated at 1050 ◦C for 2h is characterized by EBSD to reveal the phases, grain size, dislocation and texture, which is shown in Fig. 1(a–c). The phase map is shown in Fig. 1(a) with face-centered cubic (FCC) γ austenite as the matrix embedded with a small fraction of body-centered cubic (BCC) α phase. The volume fraction of the α phase is approximately 3 %, with an average grain size of about 4.7 μm, uniformly distributed along the austenite grain boundaries. It can be seen that equiaxed γ grain structures included many annealing twin boundaries depicted by white color. The average grain size defined with misorientation angle no less than $_ { 1 5 ^ { \circ } }$ is about 10.8 μm. The dislocation density in this starting material is revealed by the KAM values in Fig. 1(b), which is related to the geometric necessary dislocation (GND) and its average density is measured to be is $1 . 6 \times \dot { 1 } 0 ^ { 1 3 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 2 }$ . The inverse pole figure (IPF) map along the forging direction is shown in Fig. 1(c), indicating a very weak texture in the starting material.

The microstructure of the starting materials after solution-treated at 1050 ◦C for 2 h is characterized by TEM to reveal the possible nanoprecipitation. Selected area electron diffraction patterns along the $[ 1 0 0 ] _ { \gamma } , [ 1 1 0 ] _ { \gamma } ,$ and [110]MC zone axis, are shown in Fig. 1 (d), (e) and (f), respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 1(d) that the extremely weak superlattice spots near the [0-11] diffraction patterns are observed, which cannot be observed in the corresponding dark field image. This weak superlattice diffraction spots have been revealed to be an $\mathbf { L } 1 _ { 2 }$ type short-range ordering (SRO) in previous studies [3,14,21,27,38]. Satellite reflections can be observed near some of the main γ diffraction spots. Some previous studies indicated that satellite reflections adjacent to the {200}γ and {220}γ primary diffraction spots originate from lattice modulation caused by concentration fluctuations of solute Al and C atoms [21,39]. Furthermore, based on examination of the precipitated phase by the electrolytic extraction method, it is found that V is completely dissolved in the steel, Nb, Mo and W is partially dissolved in the steel, with approximately 0.047 (wt.%) Nb, 0.017 (wt.%) Mo, and 0.024 (wt.%) W existing in MC carbide.

Such twinning observed in this high SFE steel is similar to that found in Cu–10Mn (at. %) alloy [31], Fe-22Mn-x $\left( \mathbf { x } = 0 , 3 , 6 \right) \mathrm { A l } \mathbf { - } 0 . 6 \mathrm { C }$ [62] and Fe30Mn11Al1.2C(wt.%) steels [27]. Twinning cannot be activated for copper in traditional deformation processes, but the incorporation of 10 at. % Mn can induce twin formation. The authors attributed this to the presence of SRO, which promotes planar slip causing that critical twinning stress can be easily reached by stress concentration [31]. Park et al. [62] also attributed planar slip to the glide softening associated with SRO. In addition, they have qualitatively proved that the interaction between dislocation and SRO can lower the critical stress for twinning in the Fe-22Mn-x $( \mathbf { x } = 3 , 6 ) \ : \mathrm { A l } – 0 . 6 \mathrm { C }$ steel [62]. Ren et al. [27] obtained similar findings that the high flow stress induced by dislocation planar slip and the reduction in critical twinning stress due to the presence of SRO may be the causes of the twinning phenomenon. In current work, the microstructure contains SRO (Fig. 1(d)). Furthermore, no typical mechanical antiphase domain boundaries (APBs) bounded by two partials are observed in this steel. The short-range order in our designed steel is similar to that of Fe-22Mn-x (x = 3,6) Al-0.6C steel [62]. Thus, the short-range order in our steel may reduce the stacking fault energy and critical twinning stress rather than increasing them. Type II and III grains form cell block walls with planar slip characteristics, which lead to stress concentration required for twinning nucleation and growth (Fig. 7(g–k)). Type I grains form uniform dislocation cells, which are not conducive to the formation of the high stress required for twinning growth. The shear stress arising from stress concentration at these cell block walls significantly exceeds the macroscopic shear stress. The homogeneous dislocation cell structure in type I grains effectively disperses the macroscopic shear stress. Thus, type II and III grains, which are not favorable for twin activation, paradoxically generate a substantial number of twins. Conversely, type I grains, which are conducive to twin initiation, only produce a small number of twins at the grain boundaries, which are governed by the twin nucleation stress. Therefore, the high stacking fault energy (SFE) twinning and its orientation dependence are mainly attributed to the stress concentration induced by planar dislocation substructure.

caption: Fig. 2. Mechanical properties at RT and LNT: (a) Tensile engineering stress-strain curves. (b) True stress-strain curves and corresponding strain hardening rate curves. (c) Force-displacement curves and energy-displacement curves.
paper_abstract: Qingfeng Kang , Zexi Zhang , Ziyuan Gao , Cunyu Wang , Jianxiong Liang , Zhengdong Liu , Yuqing Weng , Wenquan Cao D
paper_title: Excellent mechanical properties and underlined deformation twinning mechanism of a low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel
citation_sentences: The true stress-strain curves along with the corresponding strain hardening rate curves at RT and LNT were displayed in ${ \mathrm { F i g . ~ } } 2 \ { \mathrm { ~ ( b ) } }$ . As shown in Fig. 2 (b), the strain hardening curves at RT and LNT can be divided into two and four stages, respectively, which indicates that the hardening behavior at RT and LNT is governed by different mechanisms, which will be interpreted based on the microstructure examination in the discussion section. To further understand the differences in impact toughness at RT and LNT, the instrumented impact test was applied to reveal the different impact energy contribution from the crack initiation and crack propagation. The results were displaced in Fig. 2(c) by the impact force-displacement curves and energy-displacement curves, in which the impact energy was calculated by the integration of the forcedisplacement curve, which was taken as the impact energy. The crack initiation energy $W _ { m }$ is defined by the integration of the forcedisplacement curve from zero force to the maximum force Fm, and the crack propagation energy $W _ { p }$ is defined by the integration of the forcedisplacement curve from the maximum force Fm to the fracture force. The total impact energy is calculated as the sum of crack initiation energy and the crack propagation energy as shown in Fig. 2(c). It is very interesting that crack initiation is almost the same of \~80–100 J for both room temperature deformation and cryogenic temperature deformation. However, the crack propagation energy is changed significantly from 251 J at room temperature to 156 J at cryogenic temperature. This means that the Charpy impact at both temperature is governed by crack propagation energy.

As it is shown in Fig. 2(b), the work hardening rate in stage A is sharply decreased with increasing of tensile strain at both RT and LNT. This sharp decreasing phenomenon could be easily interpreted by the dislocation accumulation and recovery [30,61]. However, the work hardening rate at LNT is much higher than that at RT, which could be attributed to the increased lattice friction stress with decreasing of deformation temperature as reported found in TWIP steel [30]. This explanation is consistent with our calculation that the lattice friction stresses are 143 MPa for RT deformation but 389 MPa for LNT deformation as revealed in subsequent calculations.

citation_sentences: The texture evolutions of austenite along tensile axis (tensile axis is shorten as TA) during tensile deformation at RT and LNT are examined by EBSD and the results shown in Fig. 3(a) and (b). The IPF of the tensile samples at room temperature are calculated from the orientation data measured by an in-situ EBSD tension experiments. It can be seen that a weak texture of the austenite as shown in Fig. 3(a) before the tensile test, but the texture strength increases gradually with increasing of the tensile deformation strain. Finally, a very strong <111>//TA texture and weak ${ < } 1 0 0 { > } / / \mathrm { T A }$ texture were observed in the samples tensile deformed at RT as shown in Fig. 3(a). Similar texture evolution is found in the samples tensile deformed at cryogenic temperature as shown in Fig. 3(b). It should be pointed out, the texture strength of <111>//TA texture component at room temperature is slightly higher than that deformed at cryogenic temperature. However, the texture strength of <111>//TA texture component at the maximum uniform elongation at cryogenic temperature is significantly higher than that deformed at room temperature, which is clearly resulted from the higher tensile deformation strain at cryogenic temperature.

(a2, b2, c2, d2) Inverse pole maps along the TA direction. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

citation_sentences: Fig. 5(b1, c1, d1) illustrates the correlation between deformation twinning and grain orientation at 28 %, 38 %, and 55 % engineering strain at LNT, respectively. Similar to room temperature deformation, twins are more readily formed in type II and type III grains. As shown in Fig. 5(b1, b2, c1, c2, d1, d2), plenty deformation twins could be found in the microstructure when the studied steel is deformed at LNT. And the deformation twinning fraction increased with the increasing of the deformation strain. Thus, the deformation twinning plays an important role in the deformation behaviors of our studied low-carbon low-density steel at LNT temperature, which is strongly dependent on the grain orientations. It can be observed in Fig. 5(c2) that type II grains tend to form single orientation twinning, while type III grains tend to form multiple orientation twinning.

Although deformation twins paralleling to the primary slip plane are further developed at 38 % strain level, the proportion of deformation twins is still very low (Fig. 7(k, l)), which is consistent with the finding by EBSD as shown in Fig. 5(a1, a2).

where $\tau _ { t w / s }$ is twin stress/dislocation slip stress, $m _ { t w / s } =$ cos $\phi$ cos λ is the twin/dislocation slip Schmid factor, σ is the macroscopic stress in MPa, Φ is the angle between the twinning plane normal/dislocation slip plane normal and the tensile axis, and λ is the angle between the twinning shear direction/dislocation slip direction and the tensile axis. It can be observed that massive twinning is activated in the grains with orientation type III assume higher twinning Schmid factor than that of dislocation Schmid factor. However, Schmid law cannot explain the twinning behavior of type I and II grains. Only a small number of deformation twinning is formed at grain boundary in type I grains, though their Schmid factor for dislocation gliding is lower than that for deformation twinning activation. Type II grains do not obey Schmid law, but they do exhibit a large amount of deformation twinning. Gutierrez-Urrutia and Raabe attributed the deformation twinning in these unfavorably oriented grains (type II) in TWIP steel to stress concentrations induced by the impact of twins in neighboring grains [58]. However, they did not consider the influence of dislocation structures on the flow stress. In fact, type II grains, which generate twins, are scarcely impacted by the twins of adjacent grains, as displayed in Fig. 5(c1-c3).

paper_abstract: Qingfeng Kang , Zexi Zhang , Ziyuan Gao , Cunyu Wang , Jianxiong Liang , Zhengdong Liu , Yuqing Weng , Wenquan Cao D
paper_title: Excellent mechanical properties and underlined deformation twinning mechanism of a low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel
citation_sentences: For the understanding of the deformation behaviors of our studied steel at RT, the deformation microstructures are examined by TEM after different deformation strain and the results are displayed in Fig. 7. It can be seen, when the strain reaches to 3 %, complex dislocation configurations are formed, such as dislocation nodes, multiple dislocations and dislocation pairs (Fig. 7(a)), which are supposed to be related to planar slip [21]. After 10 % deformation, Lomer-Cottrell (LC) locks and the high-density dislocation walls (HDDWs) are formed (Fig. 7(b and c)), which are regularly observed in other FCC materials [21,42]. After 20 % strain, the so-called Taylor lattices, cell blocks (CBs) and dislocation cells (DCs) are formed along {111} slip plane (Fig. 7(d–f)). It should be noted that Taylor lattice is not popular compared to dislocation cell and cell block. This implies that dislocations are more prone to cross slip. Furthermore, grains (type I) oriented close to ${ < } 1 0 0 { > } / / \mathrm { T A }$ are more prone to forming dislocation cells (Fig. 7(f)), while grains (type II and III) oriented away from ${ < } 1 0 0 { > } / / \mathrm { T A }$ are more likely to develop cell blocks (Fig. 7(e)). Fig. 7(g–j) shows that the CBs and DCs are refined as the strain increases to 28 %. The refinement of cell blocks also has been observed in TWIP and lightweight steels [40,41]. A small amount of deformation twinning has been formed along the cell block walls (CBWs), as shown in Fig. 7(g and h). Furthermore, a few microbands (Fig. 7(j)) are found, which is an indication of stress localization.

Although deformation twins paralleling to the primary slip plane are further developed at 38 % strain level, the proportion of deformation twins is still very low (Fig. 7(k, l)), which is consistent with the finding by EBSD as shown in Fig. 5(a1, a2).

Based on the calculation of the stacking fault energy, the phase fraction at LNT and the phase diagram as shown above, the stability of our designed low-carbon low-density steel is very high, which theoretically cannot be deformed by deformation twinning and phase transformation from austenite to martensite. However, deformation twins are taken place both at RT and LNT, indicating a controversy between SFE prediction twins and experimental observation for our studied lowcarbon low-density steel, which is strongly dependent on the deformation strain, deformation temperature (as shown in Figs. 9 and 10), crystal orientation of the grains (as shown in Fig. 7) and will be discussed in section 4.2.

Such twinning observed in this high SFE steel is similar to that found in Cu–10Mn (at. %) alloy [31], Fe-22Mn-x $\left( \mathbf { x } = 0 , 3 , 6 \right) \mathrm { A l } \mathbf { - } 0 . 6 \mathrm { C }$ [62] and Fe30Mn11Al1.2C(wt.%) steels [27]. Twinning cannot be activated for copper in traditional deformation processes, but the incorporation of 10 at. % Mn can induce twin formation. The authors attributed this to the presence of SRO, which promotes planar slip causing that critical twinning stress can be easily reached by stress concentration [31]. Park et al. [62] also attributed planar slip to the glide softening associated with SRO. In addition, they have qualitatively proved that the interaction between dislocation and SRO can lower the critical stress for twinning in the Fe-22Mn-x $( \mathbf { x } = 3 , 6 ) \ : \mathrm { A l } – 0 . 6 \mathrm { C }$ steel [62]. Ren et al. [27] obtained similar findings that the high flow stress induced by dislocation planar slip and the reduction in critical twinning stress due to the presence of SRO may be the causes of the twinning phenomenon. In current work, the microstructure contains SRO (Fig. 1(d)). Furthermore, no typical mechanical antiphase domain boundaries (APBs) bounded by two partials are observed in this steel. The short-range order in our designed steel is similar to that of Fe-22Mn-x (x = 3,6) Al-0.6C steel [62]. Thus, the short-range order in our steel may reduce the stacking fault energy and critical twinning stress rather than increasing them. Type II and III grains form cell block walls with planar slip characteristics, which lead to stress concentration required for twinning nucleation and growth (Fig. 7(g–k)). Type I grains form uniform dislocation cells, which are not conducive to the formation of the high stress required for twinning growth. The shear stress arising from stress concentration at these cell block walls significantly exceeds the macroscopic shear stress. The homogeneous dislocation cell structure in type I grains effectively disperses the macroscopic shear stress. Thus, type II and III grains, which are not favorable for twin activation, paradoxically generate a substantial number of twins. Conversely, type I grains, which are conducive to twin initiation, only produce a small number of twins at the grain boundaries, which are governed by the twin nucleation stress. Therefore, the high stacking fault energy (SFE) twinning and its orientation dependence are mainly attributed to the stress concentration induced by planar dislocation substructure.

caption: Fig. 8. TEM images of deformation induced microstructure at different tensile strains at LNT, indicating the occurrence of deformation twin at 10 % tensile strain: (a, b) 3 %, (c–e) 10 %, (f–i) 20 %, (j–o) 28 %, (p, q) 38 %, (r–t) 55 %.
paper_abstract: Qingfeng Kang , Zexi Zhang , Ziyuan Gao , Cunyu Wang , Jianxiong Liang , Zhengdong Liu , Yuqing Weng , Wenquan Cao D
paper_title: Excellent mechanical properties and underlined deformation twinning mechanism of a low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel
citation_sentences: Deformation-induced microstructures at LNT are also examined by TEM and the results are shown in Fig. 8. Upon reaching a strain of 3 %,

complex planar slip features, dislocation pairs, nodes, and multiple dislocations emerged, as displayed in Fig. 8 (a). Unlike at room temperature, the occurrence of extended dislocation pairs at lowtemperature indicates that low temperature facilitates planar glide. It is particularly intriguing that stacking faults are formed at the annealed twin boundaries, as displayed in the inset of Fig. 8(b). This implies that low temperature has facilitated the formation of stacking faults at early deformation. At a strain of 10 %, the features of complex planar slip are further pronounced, as depicted in Fig. 8(c). Fig. 8(d and e) illustrates that deformation twins have been nucleated at annealed twin boundaries upon reaching an approximate strain of 10 %. Typically, the width of twin bundles is about 5 nm for 10 % strain (Fig. 8(e)), which cannot be detected by EBSD. It should be noted that, unlike the deformation twins formed at room temperature through high-density dislocations, these deformation twins formed at low strain are closely related to the

stacking faults formed at the annealed twin boundaries. Upon straining to 20 %, dislocation cell blocks (Fig. 8(e)), and dislocation cells (Fig. 8 (g)), are produced respectively in grains oriented away from <100>// TA and close to <100>//TA, which is similar to observation at room temperature. Furthermore, at 20 % strain, deformation twins and dislocation cells have been developed in the interior of the grains, but the proportion of twins is still very low (Fig. 8(h and i)), indicating a competition between dislocation gliding and deformation twinning in this stage.

Similar to the dislocation substructure at RT, the dislocation cell blocks are refined at 28 % strain level at LNT (Fig. 8(j)). Table 3 displays the average sizes of the dislocation substructures at room temperature and under liquid nitrogen. It can be observed that at equivalent strains, the sizes of dislocation substructures are reduced under liquid nitrogen compared to room temperature conditions (Table 3). This implies that the dislocation density is higher at liquid nitrogen temperature under the same strain conditions. Upon reaching an engineering strain of 28 %, while twins are further activated, their prevalence remains relatively low, as illustrated in Fig. 8(j and k). In addition, a small amount of wide microbands (Fig. 8(l, m)) and fine microbands $\left( \mathrm { F i g . ~ \ 8 ( n , ~ \ o ) } \right)$ are observed. The broad microbands can attain widths of approximately 390 nm, and the fine microbands measure around 137 nm in width, as shown in Fig. 8(l, n), respectively. It can be observed that these broad microbands and fine microbands originate from grain boundaries and within the grains, respectively, as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 8(l) and (o). Some studies indicate that the Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel $[ 2 0 , 4 1$ , 43], high manganese steel [29] and 316L stainless steel [44] exhibited plastic localization phenomena related to microbands during room-temperature or liquid nitrogen temperature deformation. As the strain is further increased to 38 %, a significant number of deformation twins are activated, as shown in Fig. 8(p, q). When the strain is reached 55 %, closing to the maximum force strain, a large number of secondary twins are activated, as displayed in Fig. 8(r, s). Fig. 8 (t) shows that, compared to primary deformation twinning, secondary twins contain more stacking faults.

Stage B hardening at LNT is characterized by almost constant in the strain hardening rate. For low aluminum, high carbon lightweight steels, the Taylor lattice observed at low strains is ascribed to the solute carbon effect on diminishing the frequency of dislocation cross-glide [41]. Fig. 8 (a–c) shows that the cross-slip of dislocations is notably enhanced, which diminishes the propensity for the formation of a Taylor lattice. Therefore, the formation of a Taylor lattice is not favored in the designed lightweight steel. Consequently, the approximately constant strain hardening rate exhibited during this phase cannot be explained by the Taylor lattice. At 3 % engineering strain, distinct stacking faults are produced at the annealed twin boundaries (Fig. 8(b)). Upon reaching 10 % engineering strain, pronounced deformation twins are evident at the annealed twin boundaries (Fig. 8(c)), a result of the previously formed stacking faults (Fig. 8(b)). As detailed in Section 4.2, the true stress at 10 % engineering strain has surpassed the calculated lower bound of the critical twinning stress. Furthermore, during this phase, an increasing trend in the {111} ${ < } 1 1 2 { > } _ { \eta }$ Schmid factor is observed, which augments the twinning partition stress and favors twin activation. Consequently, the nearly constant high strain hardening rate observed in this stage is primarily attributed to the activation of twin at the annealed twin boundary.

paper_abstract: Qingfeng Kang , Zexi Zhang , Ziyuan Gao , Cunyu Wang , Jianxiong Liang , Zhengdong Liu , Yuqing Weng , Wenquan Cao D
paper_title: Excellent mechanical properties and underlined deformation twinning mechanism of a low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel
citation_sentences: To further understand the suppression or elimination of the ductile to brittle transition in impact toughness from RT to LNT, the surface morphology of the impact fracture and the structure in the vicinity of the fracture at RT (Fig. 9(a–e)) and LNT (Fig. 9(f–j)) are analyzed, as shown in Fig. 9. For the RT sample, a large number of subordinate cracks and large necking are seen (Fig. 9(a)), whereas only a small number of subordinate cracks and small necking are seen in the cryogenic sample (Fig. 9(f)). This means that a multitude of subordinate cracks and large necking contribute to the high crack propagation energy at room temperature.

The EBSD characterizations are carried out in in the typical crack propagation zone, as indicated by the black dots in the inset of Fig. 9 $( { \bf b - g } )$ . To gain a deeper understanding of the substructure following impact deformation, TEM analysis is also performed, as shown in Fig. 9 (d–i). Fig. 9(b–g) illustrates that the samples are highly stable under both room temperature and liquid nitrogen temperature, with no phase transformation occurring in the austenite. Dislocation substructures at both temperatures are characterized by the dislocation cells or cell blocks (Fig. 9(d–i), which is similar to the dislocation substructure under tension. It can be observed that low temperature can promote the formation of deformation twins, as shown in Fig. 9(c, d, h, i). Although twins are more readily activated under low-temperature impact, they do not burst as in TWIP steels, leading to a non-significant contribution of twins to the impact energy. Additionally, the red arrows in Fig. 9(g) display the presence of distinct fine cracks around the voids generated by the detachment of α for cryogenic impact sample. This indicates that the $\gamma / \alpha$ interface is served as the crack initiation site for cryogenic impact sample, which may contribute to a decrease in crack propagation energy at low temperature. The impact energy is reduced about 30 % from 331J at room temperature to 254 J at liquid nitrogen temperature due to the existence of 3.0 % percent of the α-phase in the austenite matrix. $\mathrm { F i g . } 9 ( \mathbf { e } , \mathbf { j } )$ shows that the fracture surfaces are mainly composed of dimples of micrometer or sub-micrometer at RT and LNT,indicating that both temperatures are ductile fracture. Therefore, although the presence of the $\gamma / \alpha$ interface leads to a slight decrease in impact energy at low temperatures compared to room temperature, the refined austenite grains [1,29], absence of κ-carbides [5,32], and stability of austenite [29] endow this austenite-based duplex lightweight steel with high impact toughness at the both temperatures.

caption: Fig. 10. Comparison of our studied lightweight steel with reported low-carbon low-density steel [34], high carbon lightweight steel [47,48], low-carbon high manganese steel [29], TWIP steel [46], 304 stainless steels [49], 9Ni steel [51], and HEAs [50] at liquid nitrogen temperature. (a) Charpy impact energy versus specific yield strength. (b) Total elongation versus specific yield strength.
paper_abstract: Qingfeng Kang , Zexi Zhang , Ziyuan Gao , Cunyu Wang , Jianxiong Liang , Zhengdong Liu , Yuqing Weng , Wenquan Cao D
paper_title: Excellent mechanical properties and underlined deformation twinning mechanism of a low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel
citation_sentences: For the revealing of the advantages of our designed low-carbon lowdensity steel, the properties of the cryogenic steel were collected from literature and compared with our new designed steel as shown in Fig. 12. It can be seen from Fig. 10(a) that the Charpy impact energy and specific yield strength of the steel at liquid nitrogen temperature and those of other steels (converted to full size $1 0 \times 1 0 \times 5 5 \mathrm { m m } )$ . It can be observed that, compared to high carbon lightweight steel [47,48] and TWIP steel [46], the designed low-carbon lightweight steel exhibits a higher Charpy impact energy. In contrast to low-carbon high manganese steel [29] and 304-stainless steels [49], the designed low-carbon lightweight steel demonstrates a superior specific yield strength. 304-stainless steel is mechanically unstable and undergoes martensitic transformation at low temperatures, leading to a significant reduction in its impact energy. Although the duplex structure plays a crucial role in the fracture behavior of steel, the presence of a small amount of ferrite can significantly refine the austenite grains, which substantially enhances its impact toughness [29]. Moreover, compared to martensite, ferrite is more readily deformed, which may alleviate stress concentration at the $\gamma / \alpha$ interface to some extent. The strength and toughness of our designed lightweight steel are comparable to those of the Al0.1CoCrFeNi high-entropy alloy [50]. The ductility and the specific yield strength of the steel at liquid nitrogen temperature and those of other steels are shown in Fig. 10(b). The ductility of our studied steel is in the same level comparing with the austenite TWIP steel, but nearly 3 times higher than that of the 9Ni-steel, indicating a strong resistance of deformation induced rupture. Furthermore, although 9Ni-steel has been well recognized as a cryogenic alloy [51], the price of our designed low-carbon low-density steel is much lower that of the conventional 9Ni-steel, due to its low-cost elements alloying and simple high temperature solution heat treatment as revealed in the experiment section in this research. There are still challenges of high Ni alloy cost and complex heat treatment processes. Similarly, other stainless steel and high-entropy alloy is more expensive because of their high Co or Ni alloying design. Based on above comparison, the advantages of low-density, excellent cryogenic toughness and ductility and low-cost of our designed low-carbon low-- density steel are very impressive comparing with other cryogenic steels. These advantages make this steel to be one of the promising materials as cryogenic application.

caption: Fig. 11. Austenite phase stability of our studied low-carbon low-density steel checked by XRD examination and phase diagram calculation. (a) XRD profiles of the specimens after different tensile strains at LNT and (b) Phase diagram calculated by Thermal-Calc software (using TCFE12 database).
paper_abstract: Qingfeng Kang , Zexi Zhang , Ziyuan Gao , Cunyu Wang , Jianxiong Liang , Zhengdong Liu , Yuqing Weng , Wenquan Cao D
paper_title: Excellent mechanical properties and underlined deformation twinning mechanism of a low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel
citation_sentences: However, deformation twinning is not only found in the specimens tensile deformed at room temperature as shown in Figs. 4 and $^ { 8 , }$ in the specimens tensile deformed at liquid nitrogen temperature as shown in Figs. 5 and 9, and in the impact deformed specimens at both RT and LNT as shown in Fig. 11. However, due to the low content of carbon of 0.10 % and relative low aluminum content of 6 %, the stacking fault energy and the austenite stability should be assessed for the tendency of deformation twinning and or deformation induced phase transformation during deformation process.

In order to check the austenite stability of our designed low-carbon low-density steel, the phase identification is done by XRD and reexamined by the calculation of the phase diagram using thermal-Calc software. Both results are demonstrated in Fig. 11. It can be seen from Fig. 11(a) that the XRD profiles of the LNT deformed specimens do not vary with the deformation strain, showing the volume fraction of about 97 % and the existence of the BCC-phase with a small fraction. It can be seen that the volume fraction of both phase in the low-carbon lowdensity steel are very stable even when it was deformed at LNT. The stability of the phase is also proved that a small fraction of the BCC phase is presented in phase diagram as shown in Fig. 11(b).

paper_abstract: Qingfeng Kang , Zexi Zhang , Ziyuan Gao , Cunyu Wang , Jianxiong Liang , Zhengdong Liu , Yuqing Weng , Wenquan Cao D
paper_title: Excellent mechanical properties and underlined deformation twinning mechanism of a low-carbon low-density cryogenic steel
citation_sentences: For the revealing of the advantages of our designed low-carbon lowdensity steel, the properties of the cryogenic steel were collected from literature and compared with our new designed steel as shown in Fig. 12. It can be seen from Fig. 10(a) that the Charpy impact energy and specific yield strength of the steel at liquid nitrogen temperature and those of other steels (converted to full size $1 0 \times 1 0 \times 5 5 \mathrm { m m } )$ . It can be observed that, compared to high carbon lightweight steel [47,48] and TWIP steel [46], the designed low-carbon lightweight steel exhibits a higher Charpy impact energy. In contrast to low-carbon high manganese steel [29] and 304-stainless steels [49], the designed low-carbon lightweight steel demonstrates a superior specific yield strength. 304-stainless steel is mechanically unstable and undergoes martensitic transformation at low temperatures, leading to a significant reduction in its impact energy. Although the duplex structure plays a crucial role in the fracture behavior of steel, the presence of a small amount of ferrite can significantly refine the austenite grains, which substantially enhances its impact toughness [29]. Moreover, compared to martensite, ferrite is more readily deformed, which may alleviate stress concentration at the $\gamma / \alpha$ interface to some extent. The strength and toughness of our designed lightweight steel are comparable to those of the Al0.1CoCrFeNi high-entropy alloy [50]. The ductility and the specific yield strength of the steel at liquid nitrogen temperature and those of other steels are shown in Fig. 10(b). The ductility of our studied steel is in the same level comparing with the austenite TWIP steel, but nearly 3 times higher than that of the 9Ni-steel, indicating a strong resistance of deformation induced rupture. Furthermore, although 9Ni-steel has been well recognized as a cryogenic alloy [51], the price of our designed low-carbon low-density steel is much lower that of the conventional 9Ni-steel, due to its low-cost elements alloying and simple high temperature solution heat treatment as revealed in the experiment section in this research. There are still challenges of high Ni alloy cost and complex heat treatment processes. Similarly, other stainless steel and high-entropy alloy is more expensive because of their high Co or Ni alloying design. Based on above comparison, the advantages of low-density, excellent cryogenic toughness and ductility and low-cost of our designed low-carbon low-- density steel are very impressive comparing with other cryogenic steels. These advantages make this steel to be one of the promising materials as cryogenic application.

The Taylor factor M distribution sketch along the TA direction in this steel is shown in Fig. 12. It can be seen that twinning is primarily activated in high Taylor factor grains (type II and III). Therefore, it is reasonable to speculate that SFE is not the only decisive factor controlling the twinning process for our designed low-carbon low-density steel.
